working-tree-staging-and-history.label: Move a Change into History
working-tree-staging-and-history.summary: Follow one edit through the working tree, staging area, and first commit, then confirm the snapshot appears in history.

sketch0.title: The Working Tree Is Your Draft

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Read a Working-Tree Change
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

A committed `notes.txt` has a new line that is not staged. Use status and diff to inspect the working-tree change.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: The working tree is where edits live before they are selected for a commit.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

A committed `notes.txt` has a new line that is not staged. Use status and diff to inspect the working-tree change.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

0.message: Do not rewrite shared history in this Git practice task.
Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git diff -- notes.txt`, then check your answer again.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Move the Change into the Staging Area
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

Stage only `notes.txt`, then inspect the staged file name and staged diff.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git add copies the chosen working-tree content into the staging area; it does not create history yet.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.solutionCode:
git add notes.txt
git diff --staged --name-only
git diff --staged -- notes.txt
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

Stage only `notes.txt`, then inspect the staged file name and staged diff.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Record the Staged Snapshot
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

The notes change is already staged. Commit it with `Explain the staging area`, then read the latest one-line history entry.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git commit records the staged snapshot as a new history entry; unstaged work is not included.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Explain the staging area"
git log -1 --oneline
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

The notes change is already staged. Commit it with `Explain the staging area`, then read the latest one-line history entry.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -- notes.txt

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Verify the Three Areas Agree
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

The second notes snapshot is complete. Use short status and one-line log to verify a clean working tree and two commits.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: After a successful commit, the working tree and index can match HEAD again while the history keeps both snapshots.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Start study notes'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Explain the staging area'

single-choice-1.title: Identify the Working Tree
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the working tree in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about where you make changes before staging them.

help.concept: The working tree is where you make changes to files before staging.
help.hint_1: It's the directory where your project files are located.
help.hint_2: It's not the staging area or commit history.

options.a: The directory where you make changes to files
options.b: The area where changes are staged for commit

single-choice-2.title: Purpose of the Staging Area
single-choice-2.prompt: What is the purpose of the staging area in Git?
single-choice-2.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: The working tree, staging area, and commit history are separate layers; each Git action moves content across one boundary.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

options.a: To prepare changes for the next commit

multi-choice-1.title: Components of a Git Repository
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following are components of a Git repository? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: The working tree, staging area, and commit history are separate layers; each Git action moves content across one boundary.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Workflow
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps of the Git workflow in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Think about the sequence from editing to committing changes.

help.concept: The Git workflow involves editing, staging, and committing changes.
help.hint_1: Start with making changes in the working tree.

tokens.t1: Edit files in the working tree
tokens.t2: Stage changes in the staging area
tokens.t3: Commit changes to the commit history

fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Fill in the blank to stage a file for commit: `git [blank1] filename.txt`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

working-tree-intro.title: The Working Tree Is Your Draft
working-tree-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The working tree is the project you can open and edit right now. When you change a tracked file, the working tree moves ahead of the last commit.

```bash
printf '%s
' 'Open Saturdays' >> hours.txt
git status --short
```

Git reports a modified tracked file, but history has not changed. You are still working on a draft. This separation lets you revise freely before deciding what belongs in the next snapshot.

staging-area-intro.title: Stage the Change You Intend
staging-area-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The staging area is the proposed contents of the next commit. Add only the file that belongs to the current purpose:

```bash
git add hours.txt
git status --short
```

The status mark moves to the left column, showing that the change is staged. Staging does not copy the whole working tree automatically; it records the version of `hours.txt` that exists at that moment.

If you edit the file again afterward, Git can show both a staged version and a newer unstaged edit.

commit-history-intro.title: Commit a Named Snapshot
commit-history-intro.bodyMarkdown:
A commit records the staged snapshot with a message:

```bash
git commit -m "Add Saturday hours"
```

The message explains the purpose of the snapshot. A useful commit is focused: its files support one understandable change, and its message says what that change accomplishes.

Only staged content enters the commit. Untracked files and unstaged edits remain outside it.

git-status-bridge.title: Read the History You Created
git-status-bridge.bodyMarkdown:
After committing, inspect the timeline:

```bash
git log --oneline
```

The newest commit appears first with a shortened identifier and its subject. The identifier will differ between repositories, so focus on the order and message rather than memorizing a hash.

Finish with `git status --short`. No output means the working tree matches the latest commit and nothing is waiting to be recorded.
